explain.rule: English has three articles: 'a', 'an', and 'the'. Use 'a' before consonant sounds and 'an' before vowel sounds (a, e, i, o, u). Use 'a' or 'an' when you mention something for the first time or when it is one of many. Use 'the' when both speaker and listener know which specific thing you mean — because it was already mentioned, because there is only one, or because the context makes it clear. Some nouns take NO article: plural nouns used generally, uncountable nouns used generally, names of people, most countries, and meals.
explain.why_matters: Articles are one of the hardest parts of English for Hebrew speakers because Hebrew only has a definite article (ה) and no indefinite article. This means every time you use 'a' or 'an' in English, you are doing something that does not exist in Hebrew. Getting articles right makes your English sound natural and precise.
explain.hebrew_note: Hebrew has the definite article ה (ha) but NO indefinite article. When you say 'כלב' in Hebrew it can mean 'a dog' or just 'dog' depending on context. In English you must choose: 'a dog' (indefinite, first mention) or 'the dog' (definite, known). Also: Hebrew uses ה before adjectives too ('הכלב הגדול') — in English 'the' only comes once before the noun ('the big dog'). Never say 'the a dog' or 'a the dog'.
explain.tip: Ask yourself two questions: (1) Do we both know WHICH one I'm talking about? If yes → the. If no → a/an. (2) Does it start with a vowel SOUND? If yes → an. Note: it is the SOUND that matters, not the spelling. 'An hour' (h is silent, sounds like 'our'). 'A university' (sounds like 'you-niversity' — consonant sound).

form.a_an: Use before singular countable nouns when not specific: a book, an apple, a university, an hour
form.the: Use when specific / known / unique: the book on my desk, the sun, the president
form.no_article: Use with: plural nouns generally (Dogs are friendly), uncountable nouns generally (Water is important), most names (Israel, David), meals (breakfast, lunch)

special_cases.reason: H is silent — the word starts with a vowel sound

special_cases.case: a university / a uniform / a European country
special_cases.reason: Starts with 'you' sound — consonant sound even though spelled with U

special_cases.case: the USA / the UK / the Netherlands
special_cases.reason: Countries with 'the' are usually plural or contain a common noun

special_cases.case: go to school / go to hospital / go to work
special_cases.reason: No article when referring to the purpose/function, not a specific building
Instruments take 'the', sports take no article

good_examples.sentence: I saw a dog in the park.
good_examples.audio_text: I saw a dog in the park.
good_examples.note: First mention → a dog. Then specific park already known → the park.
Second mention — we both know which dog now → the dog.
engineer starts with vowel sound 'e' → an.

good_examples.sentence: The sun rises in the east.
good_examples.audio_text: The sun rises in the east.
good_examples.note: There is only one sun → the. East as a direction → the east.

good_examples.sentence: I had a sandwich for lunch.
good_examples.audio_text: I had a sandwich for lunch.
good_examples.note: a sandwich — first mention, one of many. lunch — meal, no article.

good_examples.sentence: Can you pass me the salt, please?
good_examples.audio_text: Can you pass me the salt, please?
good_examples.note: Both people know which salt — it's on the table in front of them → the salt.

good_examples.sentence: She plays the violin and he plays football.
good_examples.audio_text: She plays the violin and he plays football.
good_examples.note: Instruments → the violin. Sports → no article.

good_examples.sentence: I went to school in Tel Aviv.
good_examples.audio_text: I went to school in Tel Aviv.

good_examples.sentence: It took an hour to get there.
good_examples.audio_text: It took an hour to get there.
good_examples.note: hour — H is silent, starts with vowel sound → an.
Doctors in general (all doctors) → no article before plural.

bad_examples.reason: Dog is singular and countable — you must use a/an or the. Never use a singular countable noun alone.
There are many engineers — she is one of them. Use a/an, not the, for jobs unless you mean one specific unique role.

bad_examples.wrong: I ate a breakfast this morning.
bad_examples.reason: Meals (breakfast, lunch, dinner) do not take an article.
Uncountable nouns used in a general sense take no article.

exercises.question: I saw ___ dog in the garden.
exercises.hint: First mention, one of many dogs → a/an
exercises.explanation: First mention of something → use a/an. dog starts with consonant sound → a.
exercises.hebrew_note: בעברית אין כתבה לאין-סופי. כלב = just כלב. באנגלית חייבים a/an

exercises.question: ___ dog I saw was a Labrador.
exercises.hint: Second mention — we already know which dog → the
exercises.explanation: The dog was mentioned before so now both speaker and listener know which one → the.

exercises.question: She is ___ architect.
exercises.hint: architect starts with vowel sound 'a' → an
exercises.explanation: Architect starts with the vowel sound 'a' → use an.

exercises.question: ___ sun is very strong in summer.
exercises.hint: There is only one sun → the
exercises.explanation: Unique things — there is only one → the sun, the moon, the earth.

exercises.question: I usually skip ___ breakfast.
exercises.hint: Meals don't take an article
exercises.explanation: Breakfast, lunch, dinner, and supper take no article when used generally.

exercises.question: She plays ___ guitar in a band.
exercises.hint: Musical instruments always take 'the'
exercises.explanation: Musical instruments always use 'the': the guitar, the piano, the violin.

exercises.question: It was ___ honour to meet her.
exercises.hint: honour — the H is silent. What sound does it start with?
exercises.explanation: honour starts with a silent H — it sounds like 'onner' → vowel sound → an.

exercises.question: He went to ___ hospital after the accident.
exercises.hint: A specific hospital is meant here → the
exercises.explanation: When we mean a specific building (he went to a particular hospital), use the.

exercises.question: ___ water in this bottle is cold.
exercises.hint: Specific water — the water in THIS bottle → the
exercises.explanation: The water is specific — we know exactly which water (the water in this bottle) → the.

exercises.question: I'd like ___ coffee, please.
exercises.hint: One cup of coffee — first mention, countable in this context → a
exercises.explanation: A coffee = one cup of coffee. Countable portion → a.

exercises.question: ___ teachers at my school are very experienced.
exercises.hint: Specific teachers — the ones at my school → the
exercises.explanation: 'At my school' makes these specific teachers → the teachers.

exercises.question: ___ dogs are loyal animals.
exercises.hint: Dogs in general — all dogs → no article before plural general nouns
exercises.explanation: General statements about a whole category use no article with plural nouns.

exercises.question: She is ___ university student.
exercises.hint: university starts with 'you' sound — consonant sound → a not an
exercises.explanation: university sounds like 'you-niversity' — starts with a consonant sound → a university.

exercises.question: He plays ___ football every weekend.
exercises.hint: Sports take no article
exercises.explanation: Sports take no article: play football, play tennis, play basketball.

exercises.question: Choose the correct sentence:
exercises.answer: I bought a new phone yesterday.
exercises.hint: First mention of the phone
exercises.explanation: First mention → a. 'I bought the new phone' would only be correct if both speaker and listener already know which phone.
distractors: I bought the new phone yesterday.
I bought an new phone yesterday.

exercises.question: She wants to be ___ doctor.
exercises.hint: One of many doctors — not specific
exercises.explanation: Jobs use a/an when talking about someone's profession: a doctor, an engineer, a teacher.

exercises.question: We had ___ dinner at a lovely restaurant.
exercises.hint: Meals take no article
exercises.explanation: Meals (breakfast, lunch, dinner) take no article. But 'a lovely dinner' is possible if describing it.

exercises.question: It's ___ European country.
exercises.hint: European sounds like 'you-ropean' — what sound does it start with?
exercises.explanation: European starts with 'you' sound — a consonant sound → a European, not an European.

exercises.question: Could you turn off ___ lights when you leave?
exercises.hint: Both speaker and listener know which lights — the ones in this room
exercises.explanation: The lights are specific and known to both people → the lights.

exercises.question: I go to ___ work by bus.
exercises.hint: work as purpose/institution → no article
exercises.explanation: Go to work, go to school, go to university, go to hospital — these institutional uses take no article.

exercises.question: Find and fix the mistake: 'I have dog at home.'
exercises.answer: I have a dog at home.
exercises.hint: Can you use a singular countable noun without an article?
exercises.explanation: Singular countable nouns must have an article (a/an/the) or a determiner. Never 'I have dog'.
exercises.hebrew_note: בעברית: יש לי כלב. באנגלית חייבים: I have A dog

exercises.question: Find and fix the mistake: 'She plays a piano very well.'
exercises.answer: She plays the piano very well.
exercises.hint: What article do we use with musical instruments?
exercises.explanation: Musical instruments always take 'the': the piano, the violin, the guitar.

exercises.question: Find and fix the mistake: 'The life is too short to be unhappy.'
exercises.answer: Life is too short to be unhappy.
exercises.hint: General statements about uncountable nouns — what article do they take?
exercises.explanation: Uncountable nouns in general statements take no article: Life, love, happiness, time.

exercises.question: Find and fix the mistake: 'I'd like an useful piece of advice.'
exercises.answer: I'd like a useful piece of advice.
exercises.hint: useful — what sound does it start with?
exercises.explanation: useful starts with 'you' sound — a consonant sound → a useful, not an useful.

exercises.question: Find and fix the mistake: 'I had the breakfast at seven this morning.'
exercises.answer: I had breakfast at seven this morning.
exercises.hint: What article rule applies to meals?
exercises.explanation: Meals take no article: breakfast, lunch, dinner, supper.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: We saw a film at the cinema.
exercises.hint: a film = first mention. the cinema = specific, known location.
exercises.explanation: a film (one of many, first mention) + at the cinema (specific known place).

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: Everest is the tallest mountain in the world.
exercises.hint: Superlatives always use 'the'. 'The world' = unique/specific.
exercises.explanation: Superlatives always take 'the': the tallest, the best, the most. The world = unique thing.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.hint: hour → silent H → vowel sound → an
exercises.explanation: hour starts with a vowel sound (silent H) → an hour.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.hint: General uncountable noun → no article
exercises.explanation: Music and jazz in general sense → no article.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: The president is visiting the country.
exercises.hint: Unique roles and specific known places → the
exercises.explanation: The president = unique role in context. The country = specific country being discussed.

speak.sentence: I saw a really interesting film at the cinema last night.
chunks: I saw a really interesting film
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Interesting' = IN-trest-ing — three syllables, not four. Don't say 'in-ter-EST-ing'. 'Cinema' = SIN-uh-muh.

speak.sentence: She plays the piano and the violin.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Piano' = pee-AN-oh — stress on second syllable. 'Violin' = vye-uh-LIN — stress on last syllable.

speak.sentence: Can you pass me the salt and the pepper, please?
chunks: the salt and the pepper, please?
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Pass' = PASS — short 'a' sound. 'Pepper' = PEP-er. 'Please' — the 'ee' sound is long.

speak.sentence: I went to school in Tel Aviv but I went to university in Jerusalem.
chunks: I went to school in Tel Aviv
but I went to university in Jerusalem.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'University' = yoo-NIV-er-sih-tee — five syllables, stress on second. 'Jerusalem' = juh-ROO-suh-lem — stress on second syllable.

speak.sentence: It took an hour and a half to drive there.
chunks: and a half to drive there.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Hour' = OW-er — sounds like 'our', H is silent → an hour. 'Half' = HAF — the L is silent.

speak.sentence: The president of the company gave a speech about the future.
chunks: gave a speech about the future.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'President' = PREZ-ih-dent. 'Speech' = SPEECH — long 'ee'. 'Future' = FYOO-cher — stress on first syllable.

speak.sentence: Dogs are loyal animals but cats are more independent.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Loyal' = LOY-ul — two syllables. 'Independent' = in-dih-PEN-dent — four syllables, stress on third.

speak.sentence: I usually have breakfast at home but today I had a coffee at a café near the office.
chunks: I usually have breakfast at home
but today I had a coffee
at a café near the office.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Breakfast' = BREK-fust — stress on first syllable. 'Café' = KA-fay or kuh-FAY. 'Office' = OFF-is.
